articles.label: Investigation
articles.headline: Escambia County's $2.3M tourism contract renewed without competitive bid
articles.dek: The county commission quietly extended its deal with a Tallahassee marketing firm last month. Public records show no other agencies were invited to pitch.
brief: Escambia County renewed a $2.3 million tourism marketing contract with Tallahassee-based Sachs Media in March without soliciting competing bids
The three-year deal was approved on the consent agenda with no public discussion, continuing a relationship that began in 2019
County purchasing policy allows no-bid renewals for 'professional services,' but critics say contracts of this size should face periodic competition
articles.body:
The item appeared on page 47 of the March 19 consent agenda, sandwiched between a drainage easement in Bellview and a fleet vehicle purchase. No commissioner pulled it for discussion. No member of the public spoke on it. In under three seconds, Escambia County renewed a $2.3 million tourism marketing contract with Sachs Media Group.

The Tallahassee firm has held the county's tourism account since 2019. This latest renewal extends the relationship through 2029—a full decade without competitive bidding.

Public records obtained by The Flightline show no evidence that county staff solicited proposals from other marketing agencies before bringing the renewal forward. No RFP was issued. No alternative firms were contacted for quotes. The agenda item simply noted that Sachs Media had "ichcontinued to meet performance expectations."

How the contract grew

Sachs Media first won the tourism account in 2019 through a competitive process. Five agencies submitted proposals. Sachs scored highest on a rubric that weighted "regional experience" and "established media relationships" heavily—criteria that favored firms with existing Florida tourism clients.

The initial contract was worth $1.4 million over three years. When it came up for renewal in 2022, the county extended it for another three years at $1.8 million. No competitive process occurred then either.

This year's renewal bumps the annual spend to roughly $767,000—a 64% increase from the original 2019 rate.

"The tourist development tax revenue has grown significantly," said Amy Thibault, director of the county's tourism development office, in an email response to questions. "Our marketing investment has scaled proportionally."

She declined a phone interview.

What the policy allows

Escambia County's purchasing policy exempts "professional services"—a category that includes marketing, legal work, architecture and engineering—from standard competitive bidding requirements. The policy states that such services "require specialized expertise that cannot be adequately evaluated through price competition alone."

The exemption is common in Florida counties. It traces to state statute 287.055, the Consultants' Competitive Negotiation Act, which was designed primarily for architecture and engineering contracts.

But the statute doesn't prohibit competition. It simply doesn't require it.

"There's nothing stopping a county from running a competitive process for marketing services," said Gwen Hallman, a procurement attorney with the Florida League of Cities. "Many do, especially for contracts above a million dollars. It's considered a best practice."

Neighboring Santa Rosa County rebid its tourism marketing contract in 2024. Bay County does so every five years. Leon County, home to Tallahassee and Sachs Media's headquarters, requires competitive solicitation for any professional services contract exceeding $500,000.

Commissioner response

Commissioner Lumon May, whose district includes downtown Pensacola and the tourism-heavy waterfront, said he was unaware the contract had been renewed until contacted by The Flightline.

"I'll be honest, I don't read every line of the consent agenda," May said. "That's a fair criticism. But staff is supposed to flag anything significant."

He said he would ask county administration to pull the contract for a formal presentation at a future meeting.

Commissioner Jeff Bergosh, who represents the north end of the county, defended the renewal in a phone interview.

"Sachs has done good work for us," Bergosh said. "Our beach visitation numbers are up. Our digital engagement is up. Why would we blow up a relationship that's working?"

Asked whether $2.3 million warranted at least exploring alternatives, Bergosh said: "You can always second-guess. But there's a cost to switching agencies too. They'd have to learn our market from scratch."

Commission Chair Steven Barry did not respond to multiple requests for comment.

What the money buys

According to contract documents, Sachs Media's scope includes digital advertising campaigns, social media management, media buying, public relations, and "brand stewardship" for Visit Pensacola, the county's tourism arm.

The firm is also responsible for the Visit Pensacola website, visitor guides, and coordination with regional tourism partners.

A 2025 performance report prepared by Sachs Media for the Tourist Development Council claims the campaigns generated 847 million digital impressions and drove a 12% increase in website traffic year over year. The report attributes $41 million in "economic impact" to marketing-influenced visits, though the methodology behind that figure is not explained in the document.

The Flightline requested the full methodology. Thibault said it was "proprietary to Sachs Media."

Industry context

Marketing professionals contacted for this story offered mixed assessments of the contract value.

"For a destination the size of Pensacola, that's not outrageous," said Dana Carpenter, president of the Florida Association of Destination Marketing Organizations. "You're competing with Destin, Panama City Beach, Gulf Shores. You need a significant media presence."

But Rick Fernandez, who runs a mid-sized advertising agency in Tampa that has pitched Florida tourism accounts, said the lack of competition raises questions.

"Any time you're spending public money at that level, you should be testing the market periodically," Fernandez said. "Agencies get comfortable. Fees creep up. Fresh eyes can find efficiencies."

He said his firm was not aware Escambia County's contract was up for renewal. "We would have bid on it," he said.

What happens next

The renewed contract runs through September 2029. It includes annual performance reviews but no automatic termination clauses tied to specific metrics.

The next opportunity for competitive bidding would be 2029—a full decade after Sachs Media first won the account.

May said he intends to propose a policy change requiring competitive solicitation for any professional services contract above $1 million, regardless of category.

"I don't know if I'll get the votes," he said. "But the public deserves to know we're getting the best value."

The next county commission meeting is May 1.

articles.label: Development
articles.headline: Who owns the empty lots on Palafox? A block-by-block accounting
articles.dek: Downtown Pensacola has at least 14 vacant parcels in its commercial core. Most belong to five property holders. Some have sat empty for a decade.
brief: At least 14 vacant parcels sit within downtown Pensacola's commercial core, with five property holders controlling the majority of the empty land.
Some lots have remained undeveloped for more than a decade despite rising property values and downtown revitalization efforts.
Property records reveal a mix of local investors, out-of-state LLCs, and one parcel tied to a bankruptcy proceeding that has stalled development since 2019.
articles.body:
Stand at the corner of Palafox and Garden on a Saturday afternoon. To your left, a brewery patio spills onto the sidewalk. To your right, a chain-link fence surrounds a gravel lot where weeds push through crushed limestone. A faded sign advertises "Future Development" with a phone number that rings to voicemail.

This is the contradiction at the center of downtown Pensacola's revival. Restaurants fill on weekends. Apartments lease before construction finishes. Yet fourteen parcels in the commercial core sit empty, some for a decade or more, their ownership scattered across a handful of names that appear again and again in Escambia County property records.

The Flightline spent three weeks mapping every vacant lot within the downtown Community Redevelopment Area, cross-referencing property appraiser data, corporate filings, and building permit records. What emerged is a portrait of concentrated ownership, patient capital, and a few legal tangles that have frozen prime real estate in place.

The five who hold the most

Five property holders control nine of the fourteen vacant parcels identified in this review.

Palafox Properties LLC, registered to a Pensacola address on Scenic Highway, owns three lots totaling 0.87 acres. The company's registered agent is Michael Belmont, a local attorney whose practice focuses on real estate transactions. The largest of these parcels, a half-acre lot at 301 S. Palafox, has been vacant since 2014 when a fire destroyed the three-story building that once housed a furniture store. Belmont did not respond to phone and email requests for comment.

Emerald Coast Holdings, a Destin-based LLC, owns two adjacent parcels on East Intendencia Street. Property records show the company purchased both lots in 2018 for a combined $1.2 million. No building permits have been pulled. Florida corporate records list the managing member as Gregory Thurston, who also holds interests in vacation rental properties along the Emerald Coast. A representative for the company said by email that "market conditions" have delayed development but declined to elaborate.

322 South Palafox LLC is registered to an address in Birmingham, Alabama. The single-member LLC owns one parcel—a quarter-acre lot that once held a gas station. Environmental records with the Florida Department of Environmental Protection show the site underwent petroleum cleanup that concluded in 2021. The lot remains empty. The registered agent, listed as CT Corporation System, is a corporate services company that handles filings for thousands of businesses nationwide. No individual owner could be identified through public records.

Robert and Catherine Hendricks, a married couple with a Pensacola address, own two lots on West Romana Street through separate LLCs. Combined, the parcels cover 0.4 acres. Building permit records show the Hendricks pulled a permit for site work in 2022 but let it expire without construction. Robert Hendricks said in a brief phone interview that rising construction costs forced them to "pause and reassess." He declined to discuss future plans.

The fifth major holder is the estate of William Jernigan, whose 0.3-acre lot at Palafox and Main has been tied up in probate since Jernigan's death in 2020. Court records show three adult children disputing the terms of a will that left the property to a family trust. A hearing is scheduled for June. None of the parties responded to requests for comment.

The rest of the map

The remaining five parcels have individual owners with no apparent connection to each other.

A lot at 118 S. Tarragona belongs to First City Bank, which acquired it through foreclosure in 2023. A bank spokesperson said the property "remains under review for disposition" but would not say whether a sale is planned.

Two small parcels on East Government Street, each under a tenth of an acre, are owned by individuals whose mailing addresses are in Atlanta and New Orleans respectively. Neither responded to certified letters sent to their addresses of record.

The final two lots sit at the northern edge of the CRA boundary on West Wright Street. One is owned by Escambia County itself—a remnant from a road-widening project in the 1990s that was never executed. County records show no current plans for the parcel. The other belongs to Sacred Heart Hospital's parent organization, Ascension, and is used informally for overflow parking during peak hours. An Ascension spokesperson said the lot is "not currently slated for development."

What the city can and cannot do

Pensacola's Community Redevelopment Agency has tools to encourage development on vacant land but limited power to compel it.

The CRA offers tax increment financing for projects that meet certain thresholds—typically mixed-use buildings with residential units above ground-floor retail. It can also forgive impact fees and expedite permitting. What it cannot do is force an owner to build or sell.

Florida law prohibits most forms of vacancy taxes or land banking penalties that cities in other states have used to discourage speculative holding. A bill in the 2024 legislative session that would have allowed local vacancy fees died in committee.

"We can make the carrot very attractive," said Pensacola CRA director Keisha Powell in an interview. "But we don't have a stick. If someone wants to sit on land and wait for values to rise, that's their right as a property owner."

Powell said her office has reached out to every vacant parcel owner in the downtown core. Some have engaged with incentive programs. Others have not responded at all.

Assessed values for the fourteen parcels have increased an average of 34 percent since 2020, according to property appraiser data. The total assessed value now exceeds $8.4 million. Annual property tax revenue from the lots—which contribute to the CRA's operating budget—amounts to approximately $112,000.

The view from the sidewalk

On a Wednesday morning, a woman named Doris Callahan stood outside the fenced lot at 301 S. Palafox, waiting for a downtown trolley. She's lived in Pensacola since 1978. She remembers when Palafox was half-empty storefronts and bail bond offices.

"It's better now, no question," she said. "But these holes in the street—they've been holes my whole life. Different holes, maybe. But always holes."

She gestured at the gravel lot behind her.

"Somebody's waiting for something," she said. "I just don't know what."

articles.label: Local News
articles.headline: Blue Angels homecoming week brings 200,000 visitors and a city stretched thin
articles.dek: With the July show ten weeks out, Pensacola Beach businesses are already short-staffed. City officials say they're still finalizing traffic and parking plans.
brief: The Blue Angels homecoming show July 10-12 is expected to draw over 200,000 visitors to Pensacola Beach and the National Naval Aviation Museum.
Beach businesses report staffing shortages of 20-30% heading into peak season, with housing costs pricing out seasonal workers.
Escambia County and city officials have yet to release final traffic management and parking plans for the three-day event.
articles.body:
The marquee at Flounders Beach Bar still advertises Easter brunch specials. Inside, owner Mike Serreyn is looking at a staffing spreadsheet that hasn't changed in three weeks.

"I need forty people for homecoming weekend," Serreyn said. "I've got twenty-six. Maybe."

Ten weeks from now, an estimated 200,000 visitors will descend on Pensacola Beach and the National Naval Aviation Museum for the Blue Angels homecoming air show. The three-day event, scheduled for July 10-12, is the largest annual draw for Escambia County tourism—and the most logistically complex weekend of the year.

Businesses are bracing. Government agencies are still planning. And the workers who make it all run are increasingly hard to find.

The math doesn't work

The Santa Rosa Island Authority, which oversees Pensacola Beach, estimates the homecoming show generates $25-30 million in direct economic impact. Hotels book solid months in advance. Restaurants run double shifts for seventy-two hours straight.

But the seasonal labor market that once staffed those shifts has fractured.

Serreyn has operated Flounders for nineteen years. He's never had trouble hiring for summer. This year, he's competing with Destin and Panama City Beach—and losing.

"Kids can't afford to live here anymore," he said. "A studio apartment on the beach is $1,800 a month. They're making $15, maybe $18 an hour. You do the math."

He's not alone. A Flightline survey of twelve beach-area restaurants and bars found staffing shortages ranging from 15% to 40% compared to the same period in 2024. Seven of the twelve said they would likely reduce hours or services during homecoming weekend.

Casino Beach Bar & Grille general manager Tammy Rodriguez said she's already planning to close two of her four service stations during the show.

"We'll do the best we can with what we've got," Rodriguez said. "But people need to understand—if you're waiting forty-five minutes for a burger, that's just how it is."

Traffic plans pending

The visitor surge creates pressure well before anyone orders a drink.

Last year's homecoming show produced a twelve-mile backup on Pensacola Beach Boulevard. Vehicles idled on the Bob Sikes Bridge for over two hours. Emergency responders reported delayed response times across the beach.

Escambia County Public Safety Director Eric Gilmore acknowledged the problems at a county commission workshop earlier this month.

"We learned some lessons last year," Gilmore said. "We're working on improvements."

What those improvements look like remains unclear. The county has not released a traffic management plan for the 2026 show. Neither has the city of Gulf Breeze, which controls access to the Bob Sikes Bridge.

Gulf Breeze City Manager Samantha Abell said her staff is coordinating with county officials but declined to provide specifics.

"We're still in the planning phase," Abell said in an email. "We expect to have something finalized by mid-June."

That timeline concerns Pensacola Beach Chamber of Commerce president Nan Harper.

"Mid-June is four weeks before the show," Harper said. "Businesses need to know what's happening so they can plan. Their employees need to know how to get to work. You can't figure this out at the last minute."

The other show

While the beach draws the largest crowds, the Blue Angels perform their full demonstration over the National Naval Aviation Museum at NAS Pensacola. That show is free and draws an estimated 80,000 spectators to the base.

Navy officials say they've improved spectator flow since last year, when gates closed two hours before the show due to overcapacity.

"We've expanded our parking footprint and adjusted our entry procedures," said Lt. Cmdr. Brian Daniels, a spokesman for Naval Air Station Pensacola. "We're confident we can handle the crowds safely."

The museum show requires base access, which means security screening for every visitor. Last year, wait times at the main gate exceeded ninety minutes.

Daniels said the base would open additional entry points this year but did not specify locations or hours.

Housing the workers

Behind the staffing numbers is a housing crisis that has been building for years.

Escambia County's median rent increased 34% between 2020 and 2025, according to U.S. Census Bureau data. On Pensacola Beach, the increase was closer to 50%. Short-term vacation rentals have absorbed much of the available housing stock, leaving seasonal workers with few options.

The Santa Rosa Island Authority considered a workforce housing proposal last fall. It went nowhere.

"There's no appetite for it," said SRIA board member Thomas Campanella. "Property owners don't want affordable housing next to their condos. It's that simple."

Some businesses have tried creative solutions. Peg Leg Pete's, the oyster bar on Via de Luna, converted a storage building into bunk housing for summer staff. Owner Renee Mack said she can house eight workers there.

"It's not ideal," Mack said. "But it's either that or we don't have cooks."

The stakes

For all the logistical headaches, no one is suggesting the homecoming show isn't worth it.

The Blue Angels have been based at NAS Pensacola since 1954. The homecoming show is a civic ritual, a military tradition, and the anchor of the summer tourism season. Canceling or scaling back is not part of the conversation.

But the gap between what the event demands and what the community can deliver is widening.

"We love the Blue Angels," Serreyn said. "This community loves the Blue Angels. But we're running on fumes here. Something's gotta give."

He looked back at his staffing spreadsheet. Twenty-six names. Maybe.

"Ten weeks," he said. "I guess we'll find out."
